data_in.description: example_counts dataset (SummarizedExperiment with peak-by-sample count matrix and rowRanges)

data_out.description: Numeric summary table with mean, median, and standard deviation of kmer variability scores for 6-mers and 7-mers
Kmer deviation object for 6-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays)
Kmer deviation object for 7-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays)

domain_knowledge: K-mer size trade-off: 6-mers are computationally faster but capture less sequence specificity; 7-mers yield higher variability and better de novo motif assembly but require more computation.
Bias correction in chromVAR accounts for GC content and average accessibility patterns; background peak sets matched on these properties normalize deviation scores to prevent confounding.

Deviation Z-scores combine effect size (deviation magnitude) with confidence (informed by read depth); higher absolute Z-scores indicate greater departure from expected accessibility, not necessarily higher raw variability.

Variability is computed as the standard deviation of Z-scores across all cells/samples for a given annotation set; this metric reflects consistency of accessibility patterns and is used to rank motifs or kmers by biological importance.

verify 7-mer annotation matrix output file_format_is matrix or SummarizedExperiment

cmd: library(chromVAR); library(motifmatchr); library(BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19); library(SummarizedExperiment); library(Matrix); library(BiocParallel); data(example_counts); counts <- example_counts; counts <- addGCBias(counts, genome=BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19); counts <- filterSamples(counts, min_depth=1500, min_in_peaks=0.15); counts <- filterPeaks(counts); kmer6_annotation <- matchKmers(6, rowRanges(counts), genome=BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19); kmer7_annotation <- matchKmers(7, rowRanges(counts), genome=BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19); dev6 <- computeDeviations(object=counts, annotations=kmer6_annotation); dev7 <- computeDeviations(object=counts, annotations=kmer7_annotation); var6 <- computeVariability(dev6); var7 <- computeVariability(dev7); summary_table <- data.frame(kmer_size=c('6-mer','7-mer'), mean_variability=c(mean(var6), mean(var7)), median_variability=c(median(var6), median(var7)), sd_variability=c(sd(var6), sd(var7))); write.csv(summary_table, 'kmer_variability_summary.csv', row.names=FALSE); saveRDS(dev6, 'kmer6_deviations.rds'); saveRDS(dev7, 'kmer7_deviations.rds'); print(summary_table)

expected_outputs: Numeric summary table with mean, median, and standard deviation of kmer variability scores for 6-mers and 7-mers
Kmer deviation object for 6-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays)
Kmer deviation object for 7-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays)
finding: chromVAR supports kmer-based annotation approaches as a viable method for analyzing chromatin accessibility variability, with kmers being suitable for deviation computation workflows.
inputs: example_counts dataset (SummarizedExperiment with peak-by-sample count matrix and rowRanges)
methodology_summary: Load and preprocess example_counts: apply GC bias annotation, filter samples (min_depth=1500, min_in_peaks=0.15), and filter peaks to remove overlaps.
Generate 6-mer and 7-mer kmer annotation matrices by scanning all peak sequences against all possible kmers of each length using matchKmers.
Compute bias-corrected deviations for each kmer set using computeDeviations, which normalizes against background peak sets matched for GC content and mean accessibility.
Compute variability (standard deviation of deviation Z-scores across all samples) for each kmer using computeVariability.
Extract and compare summary statistics (mean, median, standard deviation) of variability distributions for 6-mers vs. 7-mers.

Validation: confirm that 7-mer variability distribution exhibits higher mean and median scores than 6-mer distribution, consistent with the reported finding that 7-mers provide greater sequence specificity and superior signal for de novo motif assembly.

task_description: Assemble 6-mer and 7-mer kmer annotations from filtered peak regions using assembleKmers, compute chromatin accessibility deviations for each kmer set, and produce a summary table comparing mean and median variability scores between kmer sizes to validate the reported advantage of 7-mers over 6-mers.
task_objective: Build 6-mer and 7-mer annotation matrices on example_counts using assembleKmers, compute deviations for each, and produce a summary table comparing mean/median variability scores between kmer sizes.

task_outputs.label: Summary table comparing 6-mer and 7-mer variability statistics

title: Reproduce the finding that 7-mers yield higher variability than 6-mers in chromVAR kmer analysis

Synthesis grounding: the following tools/outputs were NOT found in the source paper and are inferred — verify before use: SummarizedExperiment, BiocParallel, BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19, Numeric summary table with mean, median, and standard deviation of kmer variability scores for 6-mers and 7-mers, Kmer deviation object for 6-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays), Kmer deviation object for 7-mers (RangedSummarizedExperiment with deviations and deviationScores assays).
workflow_description: 1. Load chromVAR, motifmatchr, SummarizedExperiment, Matrix, and BiocParallel packages; register SerialParam for single-core execution. 2. Load example_counts dataset and filter with addGCBias using BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19, then apply filterSamples with min_depth=1500 and min_in_peaks=0.15, and filterPeaks to remove overlapping peaks. 3. Generate 6-mer kmer annotation matrix using matchKmers(6, counts_filtered, genome=BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19), then compute deviations with computeDeviations(counts_filtered, kmer_ix_6mer). 4. Compute variability scores for 6-mers using computeVariability on the resulting deviation object. 5. Repeat steps 3–4 for 7-mers using matchKmers(7, counts_filtered, genome=BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19) and computeDeviations. 6. Extract and tabulate mean, median, and standard deviation of variability scores for both kmer sets, then compare distributions to confirm 7-mers yield higher variability than 6-mers.
